qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Client TLS wrappers accept unauthenticated or mismatched certificates by default
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The advisory is a server-side mTLS authentication bypass: CERT_REQUIRED failed to include SSL_VERIFY_FAIL_IF_NO_PEER_CERT, so a server accepted clients that omitted a certificate. This finding is a different client-side root cause centered on CERT_NONE defaults and missing server_hostname-driven hostname verification in wrap_socket().

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No qualifying finding identifies the advisory-described vulnerable code or root cause. The advisory fixes server-side client-certificate enforcement for CERT_REQUIRED, while the only qualifying finding describes client-side peer and hostname verification weaknesses in separate wrapper behaviors.
evidence: GHSA-vj87-jj27-4h9c states that verify_mode=CERT_REQUIRED did not fully enforce client certificates because SSL_VERIFY_FAIL_IF_NO_PEER_CERT was missing, allowing mTLS bypass when no client certificate was sent.

The upstream fix in commit b4517dece79f682a8f453abce5cfc0b81bae769d changes wolfssl/__init__.py constants so CERT_REQUIRED becomes SSL_VERIFY_PEER | SSL_VERIFY_FAIL_IF_NO_PEER_CERT and adds CERT_OPTIONAL, which is the server-side verify-mode root cause described by the advisory.

The qualifying finding instead reports that SSLContext, SSLSocket, and wrap_socket default to CERT_NONE and that wrap_socket() lacks a server_hostname parameter for hostname verification, which is a client-side MITM condition rather than a missing client-certificate enforcement flag.
No reproduction bundle or poc_report.json exists for the qualifying finding, and PoC execution is not applicable because the finding does not match the advisory root cause.
